sentences.text: Yes.
And back in the same-- I don't know whether the same block, but the similar block when I was in the first time in December.
But I was in a different condition.
I was completely gone as far as human concern.
And we were there for about one or two days.
But we already heard-- hearing the gunshots very close, and we knew that the-- the freedom, whatever that means-- meant that time is not very far.
And you come to a mental stage where you do not comprehend practically nothing.
Your state of body is unimportant.
Your mind is unimportant.
Your-- your-- your existence is unimportant.
Is nothing is important.
It just-- it's like a blur.
You know you are living, but you're not thinking.
You are a zombie-- a muselman.
And about two days, three days later, suddenly the doors open and the Russian [GERMAN], or what we call the caretakers, comes in.
And with a broken English he said, and-- no German.
It was German.
He said, [GERMAN].
And as it didn't meant anything to you, it didn't meant anything to me either.
I knew the war was over, but-- and I didn't realize until I come out from the hospital that I had a typhus, the fleck typhus, which affected everybody who had.
Because the guy said [GERMAN], and that meant the war is over.
The war is over.
And about 15 minutes later, the door opens death slowly.
I mean, I had only energy to look.
And came in an American soldier-- later on we knew he's American-- with the hat and the net, and the net is full of greens, and all the camouflage, what a front soldier wears.
But he opened the door slowly, because most probably they had fights, door to door fights.
And he comes in and then he, like this, and then he-- then he stands up, and then he seen us, and he got absolutely dead, stone dead.
He looked at us because I don't think so he believed his eyes what he saw.
We didn't see ourselves that way.
And five minutes later, soldiers poured in from everywhere-- or 10 minutes, or half an hour.
Time didn't meant anything.
And we knew that the freedom is here.
We just didn't know what freedom is.
So these poor souls, they were so shocked when they see these people.
And some people were still in a stage where they could walk, so they come in with them, [NON-ENGLISH] all around, and they ask for food, food, food, because we were beggars.

And this poor soul, he had daily rationing-- but America's soldiers get, what, piece of chocolate, a tin of sardines, or whatever it is, bread and-- they got whatever they could get out, they gave it to the [NON-ENGLISH].
And a fight broke out who get what, and they start eating.
They go out to the toilet, and they never come back.
They die there in the toilet because the food what they ate just run through the system, burning holes on-- on whatever it burns.
I don't know.
I'm not a medical man.
All I knew is we went to the toilet-- or they went.
I didn't went anywhere.
They told me that Peter, Paul, or whoever it was is just dead in the toilet.
I had the typhus, which has symptoms.
First of all, you are feverish.
Second of all, anything you touch, food, is so bitter, like poison.
And that saved my life-- again, something from upstairs, and I couldn't eat.
So I don't know when.

A day later, an hour later, or a week later, they took me to a hospital, which hospital was in the German part of-- see, Dachau was the largest-- not concentration camp, was the first concentration camp in Germany.
It was built 15 kilometer from Munich.
First for prisoner and later on for Jews and so forth.
And it was full of barracks of textile where they-- where they provided for the soldiers.
They're full of medicine.
After we got free, then we come physically, we went around to organizing-- which means stealing-- whatever we could find, eat, and clothes, and so forth.
And we find a tremendous amount of clothes, cloth-- German officer cloth-- by the 10,000 and 10,000 of meters.
And we find medicine, we don't know what.
We didn't touch.
But it was in capsules and pill forms, and buildings full of them.
So they took me to the hospital, and they started treat me as they treat a guy with fleck typhus.
And fortunately, something helped.
I don't know what was it, medicine, I don't really remember anything of that couple of weeks I spend there.
All I know is they discharged me and I was still a walking corpse, but I was already a little bit better because they fed you there.
Maybe intravenously, I don't know what they did.
I can't-- Was this the American soldiers?
This American, yeah, yeah.
Now by the time that I realized, because they never take me back to the lager where we were, they take us to the lager where the guards was staying, which mean we went into the German lager.
And the Germans were put into the lager where we were before.
And they were the prisoner, and we became free men.
And we had in the offices, which was offices during the Germans, they made accommodation, they put beds in there, blankets, and so forth.
And see, the Americans wasn't preparing for [NON-ENGLISH], they didn't know what they find.
So they supply was very limited.

But then they realized what happening in Germany, and they find-- they started rushing in from the Red Cross and from everywhere, supplies which was supposed to help the [NON-ENGLISH] and repatriate whatever they can.
So we become two or three in a room, in bed, and they established a kitchen for us.
And then came again, God.
I met a fellow who was about-- he was about four years older than I am, or five years older than I am.
He was a butcher.
His family had a butcher shop where my parents had a poultry shop.
And being a butcher, he says he's a chef, because he knew how to cook.
Most of the butchers know.
So he become the chef of the kitchen which was established for us.
And I met him, and I was a young fellow, and I know him-- it's only, what, four years when I was still a normal Jules Fischer and he was a normal [NON-ENGLISH].
So the change only happened to my system energy-wise, but my face was the same.
And he recognized me, and I recognized him.
And he said, what you doing?
I said, I just came out from the hospital.
He said, where you live?
I said, here.
He said, you come into the kitchen and you will be my assistant.
Which meant that I became a dishwasher, washing up the floors after, and he started cooking for about 3,000-4,000 people, I don't know.
It was a huge kitchen.
A lot of people worked there.
And to me he didn't let me eat the heavy food.

Because after the typhus, he cooked me soups, especially broth-- what you would call broth, we call it [NON-ENGLISH] Light stuff which I started slowly eating and started gaining my-- not energy, my weight-- and my system started getting used to food.
And from then on after, I don't know, two , three weeks later, all I seen in the hall was fire.
Everybody was cooking.
Because the chef what was making was very nice, but everybody was cooking the menu what we've talked about in the lager.
I had the bean soup.
They had the-- I had a Berliner with the eggs on the middle.
We didn't have, unfortunately, poppy seeds, so I couldn't eat poppy seed noodle.
But I stayed and ate so many eggs and Berliner you have no idea.
And that went on, and went on.
And-- and then the strength started coming back.
And then we took a train and we went into Munich.
And in Munich, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it was to us like Shangri-La.
I mean, it was something we never seen before.
But before we went to Munich, we explored the surrounding of Dachau.
And the farmers there, we went-- we cut down a piece of cloth from the depot and exchanged it for the piece of speck, or a piece of salami, or a piece of meat, or flour, or so forth.
That's how we could cook.
And nobody knew anything about what happened to us.

And we had farms, which was at the end of the lager, which mean they seen the lager, Dachau, for I don't know how many years, and they didn't see what happening inside there, which to us was unbelievable.
But--
You mean?
Yes, yes, we've seen the population of Dachau.
And we went into the city-- oh, city, sorry- village of Dachau, and we started going to places, asking for an egg, and asking for this.
And we-- we got from the Americans cigarettes, and who didn't smoke, exchange it for food.
And that exchange started between the population and the-- and the refugees, or the [NON-ENGLISH] Actually, then we become refugees.
We wasn't [NON-ENGLISH] anymore.
We were not prisoner anymore.
We were refugees, or misplaced person, or whatever it you can call it.
And we had right to go anywhere, any time.
We didn't have to pay.
We got civil uniform, and I don't think so we have any-- any identification cards at all.
We were just accepted as somebody from Dachau.
And then started-- the transports started coming in.
Romanians started taking home the Romanians.
Israel-- from Israel came transport, whoever wanted to go to Israel.
And I have a very dear friend of mine.
I only know his name, Rubian.
He was a Polish boy.
We went through a lot of things, practically, together at the end section.
So we got freed together.
And when we got freed, he wanted to go to Israel because he knew his parents don't exist anymore.
I didn't know anything about my parents.
So he went to Israel.
And we kissed, we said goodbye, never seen him anymore.
All I know is he was Rubian from Poland.
And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] came the Polish.
Not many people went back to Poland.
And then came American, who wanted to go to America, which was a bit difficult even then.
But the Hungarians never came, because Hungary was a very-- first of all, it was a collaborator country.
So nobody liked the Hungarians-- neither the Jews, nor the Yugoslavs, nor the Czechs, nor the Polish people.
The Hungarians are a very talented nation, but is not very liked by anybody.
So I got freed on the 29th of April, and we got the transport somewhere in September.
So I was in Dachau as a free man till about three or four months.
In these three or four months, we went to Munich, to Munchen.
Then I played my first football game.
The Americans organized a football match with another place which called Feldafing.
And we knew that Feldafing was a girl, a lady lagers.
So we were very anxious to get to Feldafing.
And when we got to Feldafing we realized that Feldafing was-- I don't know what sort of a lager, but it was on a lakeside, where the other side of the lake was Hitler's holiday resort.
If you read about it, that Hitler [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] keep some treasure in the lakes.
That was the lakes which was the side of Feldafing.
And Feldafing was not very far from Garmisch.
And Garmisch, even today, is one of the most expensive holiday resorts in Germany-- Garmisch-Partenkirchen they call it.
So we went to Feldafing.
The Americans took us by jeeps.
And we went, and we played against a Feldafing team.
Where they organized, I don't know.
All I know the game consisted of 22 walking zombies, because we were still zombies.
But we kicked, and that was the first football game.
And that was in 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] August.
Approximately August.
And there I met a lot of Hungarian Jews.
Ladies who were there, and Jews, and they were all much better condition than where we were.
I don't know where they were during this-- the years of the war.
But there we had the football game.
After there, we had dinner, and we got the things and we went back.
Then we went into Munich.
And one end of the Munich, I don't know what how they call it.
It was another lager in the city, and they had, it's like a hangar as well.
And also we went there, and we met other [NON-ENGLISH], and that's how our social life started slowly and slowly.
And in Auschwitz I was together with a lot of political prisoners-- communists, Hungarian communists-- who was prisoned by the Germans.
And I was together with the former Hungarian Army Minister.
His name was [NON-ENGLISH].
And the Germans deported him because he broke all the rules that he brought the Jews back from the Russian front.
Because he said that without guns they cannot serve the country, so why they are in Russia?
So the Germans deported him to Dachau, the same like they did to us.
And I got freed with him, and I was in the same wagon with him when we went back to Hungary.

And I was also together with a gentleman who became the president of the-- he formed the association in Dachau who looked after our interest towards the Americans and who-- and towards the Germans, like a committee.
And his name was Szegedy-Maszak Aladar, and he became the first ambassador-- Hungarian Ambassador to America.
So he went to America, and I don't think so he ever returned.
When the communists took over, he stayed in America.
So these were the people I got freed with.
And-- and then in September-- and I have memories a lot, it just doesn't come so well.
And in September, we got a transportation back to Hungary with exactly the same freight wagon what we left with.
The only difference was we had food with us, and the door wasn't closed.
And we went back to Hungary through Pilsen-- where you know the Pilsen beer-- and-- and then we arrived to Budapest, I don't know exactly.
All I know is I organized a top hat in Dachau.
And I was wearing a top hat because I so young, so I was still a prankster.
I wear a top hat when I first saw my mother and my father, whom I didn't know whether they're alive or not.
The only things is that I get off the train at the outskirts of Budapest, and I couldn't wait.
I took a bus.
I asked the people, how could I get to [NON-ENGLISH] where my parents' shop was?
And they told me so and so.
So I took a bus.
They didn't ask me anything, that was after war.
And they saw me, I am some strange sort of animal with a top hat on.
So I went to the market, and I went to the shop, which was confiscated from my parents when the Germans came in.
And the first person I knew, it was my mother looking at me.
And I was absolutely flabbergasted.
She wasn't.
And I tell you the reason why she wasn't.
She knew that I was alive.
And she got notice twice.
Once I wrote a letter from Auschwitz.
When I was in Auschwitz a few weeks, they gave us-- I don't know everybody-- to the children block, they distributed a postcard.
And the postcard was from Walze.
Walze was a holiday spot somewhere in Germany, somewhere around the sea area.
And on the other side you could write five words-- I am very well, kiss, your son, Jules.
And my parents received that mail in Hungary during the war, which meant that they knew I am somewhere in Germany.
Whether they knew that Auschwitz send letter under the name-- because that was the request from the Red Cross.
That's why they send.
They want to show to the Red Cross that, look, the [NON-ENGLISH] can mail to their parents, or whoever they belong to.
And the second one, in Dachau, when we freed, after about a month, a Hungarian said that he can't wait for the transport.
He start his journey back by himself.
He was a non-Jew.
He was a communist.
I'll never forget, Miklos, he was a very well built, strong guy.
He didn't go through the same things like we did.
And I told him, look, you go back to Budapest.
Maybe things is like here, people hungry.
You go to [NON-ENGLISH] and ask for Fischer Bonde at the goose-- the poultry section.
And if you find them, you tell them I'm alive, and you will get either a goose, or a chicken, or whatever they have, they will give it to you.
And when I came home, and I said to my mother, you aren't surprised that you see me?
She said, I already know that you are on the way home because Miklos was at my place.
And I gave him a goose.
And a goose in Hungary those days was to be or not to be.
How old were you at this point?
My birthday of 16 was third of May in Dachau.
And I came home, and I was 80 kilos.
And I looked like a wrestler.
And my mother asked me to go down to the cellar and bring some wood up, and try to chop up.
And I couldn't pick up an axe, because energy-- I only had fat.
I couldn't pick up an axe.
And then I said to myself, Jules, bloody things, you can't pick up an axe.
And from the next morning I started and pushups, and running, and whatever sportsman I was before needed it.
And in 1947, I start playing soccer.
And in 1948, I became a first division footballer.
In 1949 I became an international footballer.
Yes.
My parents lived in the same flat where we lived in the Jewish House.
The only thing is the rest of the families were gone.
And it was furniture in it, new furniture, my sister, who stayed with my parents right through-- she was two years older than I am-- she refurnished.
And my parents opened a shop in [NON-ENGLISH].
And by the time I came home, the home was painted and was already a normal house with Christians, and Jews, and whatever nomination you have living together.
And like nothing happens, life started.
Because life is such that bygones is bygones.
And you remember a lot of things, but what is front of you is more important than what is behind you.
So I started eating, and I started sporting.
And my parents started doing the same business what they did.
And I became a-- a-- a personality.
And through soccer, I lived a life which made up for me what I lost in-- in my German trips.
What had happened to your family?
My family was the most fortunate.
And I can say that I am one of the very, very rare Jew in the world whose family survived, every one of them-- my father's.
My mother's, they'd been persecuted and executed in a very early stage of the war.
But my father, because he was well-known, they established a squadron of forced labor guys and called a-- a squadron, which called the clothing squadron.
What was their job?
Nobody knows.
The only things they quartered themselves in the Hungarian-Jewish gymnasium-- which is not gymnasium in Hungarian terms.
It's the high school.
Gymnasium is in-- we called the high schooling gymnasium.

And with him was my whole family, about 70 or 80 of them, and another 800 Jew amongst them-- opera singers, world-famous singers who sings in the Metropolitan after that, actors, actresses, all the what you would call intellectuals-- and they bought themselves six or seven SS as a guard.
That's what I heard from my parents.
And the SS were guarding that place right through the area in such a way that no Germans and no bloody Hungarian could go near the place.
They couldn't get in.
And my sister, my cousins, who are all here, they were all hiding there, and all 700 or 800 persons survived.
And after the war, they paid the SS, and they left, and my parents came out and started their living.
So in that respect, I can say I was the sole persecuted in my family.
Sure.
Without-- I was so young, and maybe my system is such, mentally and physically, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm an individual.
And I've always been, and if God give me strength, I always will be.
And when everybody wears jeans, I'll wear tuxedo.
And when everybody wear tuxedo, I wear jeans.
I never felt myself as one of the mob.
So I hate-- my hate is specified.
I don't hate globally, and I don't hate generally.
I hate x and y.
And I show my hate, and I give it to them.
And when I played for Hakoah, somebody called me, on the field, a bloody Jew.
And the guy don't speak since then because I broke-- I-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I punched his face.
I broke his face to pieces.
And I was arrested, and Hakoah Soccer Club is the witness.
I got that arrested on the field, and I was walking on air between four policemen in Marks Field in Sydney because he called me a bloody Jew.
I don't hold against the Dutch, because the Dutch never been anti-Semite.
But that guy was.
So my hate is different from other people's hate.
I was young, my thoughts were different from a person whose whole family was destroyed, whose father, mother, sister, and all, died.
I don't blame him for his hate.
My hate is different.
I am-- I am a rare bird in the-- in the-- in the German tour.
If somebody calls me, I'll give.
I don't ask somebody else to do it for me.
And if somebody called me something today, I give it immediately.
I'm 66, and even when I'll be 100, and somebody calls me a bloody Jew, I still will whack him on the nose.
So it is-- it is something which I have to live with.
And my conscience is such that I have no bad dreams about it.
I sheltered my children-- sorry, my child-- and I sheltered my grandchild-- grandchildren.
They know about it.
She asked me.
Maybe what I'm doing is wrong.
They know.
They are absolutely familiar, but they are not familiar with my emotional side.
They familiar with the facts, not with the emotional facts.
So if that tape, and I get it, I will show them, they will know the fact, they will never know the emotional side of it.
Because my parents sheltered me when I was little.
I feel my duty shelter them when they are little.
And-- and they have to know.
They wouldn't be able to feel, so they don't have to feel.
They just have to know for future generations to come.
OK.
--Because I sold my business.
